content.problemStatement:
Check if the given binary tree is a valid Binary Search Tree (BST).

A valid BST adheres to the following criteria:


The left subtree of a node comprises only nodes with keys less than the node's key.
The right subtree of a node comprises only nodes with keys greater than the node's key.
Both the left and right subtrees must also be valid Binary Search Trees.
For eg., the given binary tree is a valid BST.




Input Format

The first line of the input contains s single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary search tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on a single line - `YES` if the given binary tree is a valid BST, else `NO`.
constraints: All pi and ci are distinct within a test case
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: The first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the tree. The next N-1 lines each contain three space-separated values pi ci Ri, where pi is the parent node value, ci is the child node value, and Ri is either 'L' (ci is the left child of pi) or 'R' (ci is the right child of pi).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output 'YES' if the described tree satisfies the Binary Search Tree properties, otherwise output 'NO'.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case forms a perfect BST (root 4, left subtree {2,1,3}, right subtree {6,5,7}). Second test case violates BST property because node 5 is in the left subtree of node 2 but is greater than 2.
A single node tree trivially satisfies BST conditions.
Node 15 is a left child of 10 but is greater than 10, breaking the BST rule.

solution.approach: To determine if a given binary tree is a valid Binary Search Tree (BST), we need to check if for every node, all elements in its left subtree are less than the node, and all elements in its right subtree are greater than the node. This can be achieved by performing a depth-first search (DFS) traversal of the tree and checking the BST property at each node.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of nodes in the tree. The DFS traversal takes O(N) time for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the tree nodes and their children.
